title: Quick check: what makes an incident Critical

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the four Critical markers named in the lesson?

choices.label: Ransomware canary trip.
choices.rationale: Named marker. Canary trips are Critical by default.

choices.label: Mass deletion of files at a rate that doesn't match user behaviour.
choices.rationale: Named marker. Data wipes, backup destruction, log destruction.

choices: A failed login on a standard user account.

choices.label: Admin-credential abuse, service-account or domain-admin credentials used in ways that don't match the legitimate workflow.
choices.rationale: Named marker. Blast radius equals the credential's privilege level.

questions.explanation: A single failed login isn't in the Critical-marker cluster. The four are canary trip, mass deletion, lateral movement, admin-credential abuse.

questions.prompt: How does the response model change when an incident is Critical?
choices: Time matters more than completeness; containment and escalation happen in parallel; you don't investigate.

choices.label: Completeness matters more; Critical means the most evidence to review carefully before acting.
choices.rationale: Inverted. Thoroughness on a Low becomes a time-cost on a Critical.

choices.label: Same shape as a High, just with a tighter SLA.
choices.rationale: The shape itself differs. Critical involves containing and handing off; High involves the full workflow.

choices.label: The tech investigates more deeply before involving a senior.
choices.rationale: The opposite of the rule. Investigation is above the helpdesk ceiling on Critical.

questions.explanation: Three shifts the lesson names explicitly. A 30-second containment that's slightly imperfect beats a 5-minute containment that's exactly right.

questions.prompt: Why is lateral movement classified Critical rather than High, even when only one host has been hit so far?
choices: The blast radius is widening in real time, credentials are being used from unexpected endpoints and the next host could appear in the next minute.

choices.label: Lateral movement always involves a domain controller.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Lateral movement can target any host.

choices.label: The SOC re-classifies all Highs to Critical after 24 hours.
choices.rationale: Fabricated mechanic.

choices.label: Lateral movement is always confirmed ransomware.
choices.rationale: Different attack shape. Lateral movement can be a precursor to many outcomes.

questions.explanation: Lateral movement is Critical because the attacker's footprint is expanding live.

questions.prompt: Mid-triage on a High EDR incident you spot Critical-shaped markers in the Evidence, admin credential abuse plus lateral movement. The incident is still tagged High. What does the lesson say to do?
choices: Bump to senior and treat as Critical, isolate the host. Don't wait for the SOC to re-tag, the pattern is the trigger and the tag follows the action.

choices.label: Re-grade the severity to Critical yourself in the portal.
choices.rationale: Severity is the SOC's call. Bump rather than re-grade.

choices.label: Finish the High workflow as recommended, the SOC tagged it High for a reason.
choices.rationale: The High tag was what the SOC had when the report opened; you now have new context from the Evidence.

choices.label: Reply to the SOC asking 'should this be Critical?' before doing anything else.
choices.rationale: Defensible to send in parallel, but containment doesn't wait for re-tagging.

questions.explanation: Recognising the pattern is the tech's contribution. The response model switches the moment the markers are clear, regardless of the incoming tag.

questions.prompt: Which Recommendation phrasing is a tell that you're looking at a Critical incident, even if you missed the severity tag?
choices: Recommendation leads with 'isolate the host immediately' or mentions 'preserve evidence' or 'do not perform further actions until forensic review'.

choices.label: Recommendation says 'approve the autoruns remediation'.
choices.rationale: Standard High-shape language. Not a Critical tell on its own.

choices.label: Recommendation says 'no action required, closing as benign'.
choices.rationale: The opposite, a Low-shape recommendation.

choices.label: Recommendation says 'verify the affected host before approving'.
choices.rationale: Routine verification language, not a Critical-specific tell.

questions.explanation: Those phrases signal that the SOC has classified the activity as needing containment now and that the deeper response is above the helpdesk ceiling.

questions.prompt: Order the helpdesk's response actions on a Critical incident.
items: Contain, isolate the host or run the ITDR compromise playbook on the affected identity.
Notify the on-call senior in parallel.
Document what you've done in the PSA.
Pass the deeper investigation to whoever owns it.
questions.explanation: Containment is first because containment is fast. Escalation runs in parallel with it. Documentation is the live record while the senior takes the deeper response.

questions.prompt: "If I'm careful, I can handle a Critical end-to-end like a High." What does the lesson say?
choices: The escalation ceiling rules it out regardless of skill level. Critical investigation is above the line because the blast radius and stakes are different, not because of how careful the tech is.

choices.label: Correct if the tech has been on the platform for at least 12 months.
choices.rationale: The ceiling isn't time-on-platform-gated.

choices.label: Correct if the senior is unreachable.
choices.rationale: Unreachable senior means escalate up the on-call chain, not take the investigation over.

choices.label: Correct as long as the tech documents thoroughly.
choices.rationale: Documentation doesn't change the scope rule.

questions.explanation: The ceiling is a scope rule, not a skill gate.

questions.prompt: "Containment will look like over-reacting if it turns out to be benign." Why does the lesson reject this framing?
choices: The cost asymmetry is the whole reason Critical exists as a category. The customer conversation after a benign isolation is much easier than after an un-contained spread.

choices.label: Customers aren't told when an isolation happens, so the appearance doesn't matter.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. Customers are informed.

choices.label: Huntress refunds the isolation if it turns out to be benign.
choices.rationale: Fabricated commercial mechanic.

choices.label: The SOC only raises Criticals when they're certain it's malicious.
choices.rationale: Overstates the SOC's certainty. Critical is raised when activity needs containment now.

questions.explanation: Cost asymmetry, the price of a benign isolation is dramatically lower than the price of un-contained spread.

questions.prompt: You've isolated and bumped the senior on a Critical. The senior confirms and says: "I'll take it from here, you stay on it for the comms side. Don't approve any further remediations." A previously queued autoruns remediation on the same host is still sitting in the workflow. What do you do?

choices.label: Continue with the autoruns remediation, the senior didn't explicitly say to stop that one.
choices.rationale: Worst response. 'Don't approve any further remediations' means all of them, including queued ones.

choices: Pause all remediation actions. Handle customer comms per the senior's direction. Document the timeline in the PSA.

choices.label: Approve the autoruns remediation but not anything else, on the grounds it was already queued.
choices.rationale: Same family of mistake. The queue state from before the Critical doesn't override the senior's current direction.

choices.label: Ask the senior whether the autoruns remediation counts as 'further'.
choices.rationale: Over-clarifying. The senior was unambiguous; act on the direction.

questions.explanation: The senior is now driving. You're supporting. Don't free-lance remediations under their direction.

questions.prompt: Critical means the most evidence to review carefully before acting.
questions.explanation: Opposite. Critical means contain first and read evidence only as context for what was contained. Thoroughness on a Low becomes a time-cost on a Critical.
